revision.comment: 649年から685年まで日本で用いられた冠位。下から2番目、後に下から3番目
revision.text:
'''小乙下'''（しょうおつげ）は、[[649年]]から[[685年]]まで日本で用いられた[[冠位]]である。[[664年]]を境に順位が変わり、下から2番目か3番目にあたる低い冠位であった。上は[[小乙上]]から[[小乙中]]、下は[[立身]]から[[大建]]に変わった。
== 概要 == [[大化]]5年2月に、冠位19階の制で導入された<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（[[649年]]）2月条。以下、特に記さない限り『日本書紀』の該当年月条による。</ref>。前の13階制の[[小黒]]が[[小乙上]]と小乙下に分割されたうちの一つである。小乙下は19階中18位で、最下位は[[立身]]であった。
天智天皇3年（[[664年]]）2月9日の冠位26階の制で、小乙は上・中・下に三分されることになった。小乙下は26階中24階で下から3番目になり、上は[[小乙中]]、下は[[大建]]となった。
天武天皇14年（[[685年]]）に冠位の命名方法が一新し、そのとき小乙下も廃止になった
== 叙位された人物 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では三つの記事でこの冠位を授かった人物が現れる。[[中臣間人老]]は、[[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月の遣唐使の一員である。[[沙尼具那]]と[[青蒜]]は斉明天皇4年（658年）の[[阿倍比羅夫]]北征で降った[[蝦夷]]で、都に上って饗応され、冠位を授けられた。[[都岐沙羅柵]]と[[渟足柵]]の[[柵造]]も同じ冠位になった。遣多禰島小使の[[上光欠]]は、大使で[[大乙下]]の[[倭馬飼部連]]とともに[[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）に[[多禰国|多禰島]]に派遣された。
*[[中臣間人老]] - [[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月見。遣唐判官。 *[[沙尼具那]] - [[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）7月4日叙位。[[渟代郡]][[大領]]。 *[[青蒜]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[津軽郡]][[少領]]。 *不詳 - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[都岐沙羅柵]]造。 *[[大伴稲積]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[渟足柵]]造。 *[[上光父]] - [[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）11月23日見。遣多禰島小使。
== 木簡に記された冠位 == [[飛鳥京跡]]から出土した天武天皇の時代の[[木簡]]の中に2つ、小乙下と記されたものがある。一つは1975年度の第51調査で見つかったもので、「小乙下階」とあった。一緒に「[[小山上]]」「[[大花下]]」と記された木簡も発見され、大花下の存続時期から、[[649年]]から[[664年]]の間というところまで絞り込める<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」100-102頁。</ref>。
もう一つは削り屑に「小乙下」とあるもので、1984年度の第104次調査で見付かった。「[[大乙下]]」、「辛巳年」「閏月」といった削り屑も一緒に出た。天武天皇10年（[[681年]]）の干支が辛巳年で閏月も持つ<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」104頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」、平野邦雄・鈴木靖民・編『木簡が語る古代史』上（都の変遷と暮らし）、吉川弘文館、1996年、ISBN 4-642-07492-9。
== 関連項目 == *[[冠位・官位制度の変遷]] [[Category:日本の位階|しようおつけ]]

revision.comment: /* 叙位された人物 */ 誤字、リンク付け
revision.text:
'''小乙下'''（しょうおつげ）は、[[649年]]から[[685年]]まで日本で用いられた[[冠位]]である。[[664年]]を境に順位が変わり、下から2番目か3番目にあたる低い冠位であった。上は[[小乙上]]から[[小乙中]]、下は[[立身]]から[[大建]]に変わった。
== 概要 == [[大化]]5年2月に、冠位19階の制で導入された<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（[[649年]]）2月条。以下、特に記さない限り『日本書紀』の該当年月条による。</ref>。前の13階制の[[小黒]]が[[小乙上]]と小乙下に分割されたうちの一つである。小乙下は19階中18位で、最下位は[[立身]]であった。
天智天皇3年（[[664年]]）2月9日の冠位26階の制で、小乙は上・中・下に三分されることになった。小乙下は26階中24階で下から3番目になり、上は[[小乙中]]、下は[[大建]]となった。
天武天皇14年（[[685年]]）に冠位の命名方法が一新し、そのとき小乙下も廃止になった
== 叙位された人物 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では三つの記事でこの冠位を授かった人物が現れる。[[中臣間人老]]は、[[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月の[[遣唐使]]の一員である。[[沙尼具那]]と[[青蒜]]は[[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）の[[阿倍比羅夫]]北征で降った[[蝦夷]]で、都に上って饗応され、冠位を授けられた。[[都岐沙羅柵]]と[[渟足柵]]の[[柵造]]も同じ冠位になった。遣多禰島小使の[[上光父]]は、大使で[[大乙下]]の[[倭馬飼部連]]とともに[[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）に[[多禰国|多禰島]]に派遣された。
*[[中臣間人老]] - [[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月見。遣唐判官。 *[[沙尼具那]] - [[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）7月4日叙位。[[渟代郡]][[大領]]。 *[[青蒜]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[津軽郡]][[少領]]。 *不詳 - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[都岐沙羅柵]]造。 *[[大伴稲積]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[渟足柵]]造。 *[[上光父]] - [[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）11月23日見。遣多禰島小使。
== 木簡に記された冠位 == [[飛鳥京跡]]から出土した天武天皇の時代の[[木簡]]の中に2つ、小乙下と記されたものがある。一つは1975年度の第51調査で見つかったもので、「小乙下階」とあった。一緒に「[[小山上]]」「[[大花下]]」と記された木簡も発見され、大花下の存続時期から、[[649年]]から[[664年]]の間というところまで絞り込める<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」100-102頁。</ref>。
もう一つは削り屑に「小乙下」とあるもので、1984年度の第104次調査で見付かった。「[[大乙下]]」、「辛巳年」「閏月」といった削り屑も一緒に出た。天武天皇10年（[[681年]]）の干支が辛巳年で閏月も持つ<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」104頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」、平野邦雄・鈴木靖民・編『木簡が語る古代史』上（都の変遷と暮らし）、吉川弘文館、1996年、ISBN 4-642-07492-9。
== 関連項目 == *[[冠位・官位制度の変遷]] [[Category:日本の位階|しようおつけ]]

revision.text:
'''小乙下'''（しょうおつげ）は、[[649年]]から[[685年]]まで日本で用いられた[[冠位]]である。[[664年]]を境に順位が変わり、下から2番目か3番目にあたる低い冠位であった。上は[[小乙上]]から[[小乙中]]、下は[[立身]]から[[大建]]に変わった。

また、『[[常陸国風土記]]』には、[[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）に[[大建]]の那珂[[国造]][[壬生夫子]]とともに[[行方郡]]新設に携わった茨木国造の[[壬生麿]]が、小乙下とある<ref>『常陸国風土記』行方郡の項。</ref>。
*[[壬生麿]] - [[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）見。茨木国造。 *[[中臣間人老]] - [[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月見。遣唐判官。 *[[沙尼具那]] - [[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）7月4日叙位。[[渟代郡]][[大領]]。 *[[青蒜]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[津軽郡]][[少領]]。 *不詳 - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[都岐沙羅柵]]造。 *[[大伴稲積]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[渟足柵]]造。 *[[上光父]] - [[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）11月23日見。遣多禰島小使。

もう一つは削り屑に「小乙下」とあるもので、1984年度の第104次調査で見付かった。「[[大乙下]]」、「辛巳年」「閏月」といった削り屑も一緒に出た。天武天皇10年（[[681年]]）の干支が辛巳年で閏月も持つ<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」104頁。</ref>。

'''小乙下'''（しょうおつげ）は、[[649年]]から[[685年]]まで日本で用いられた[[冠位]]である。[[664年]]を境に順位が変わり、下から2番目か3番目にあたる低い冠位であった。上は[[小乙上]]から[[小乙中]]、下は[[立身]]から[[大建]]に変わった。
== 概要 == [[大化]]5年2月に、冠位19階の制で導入された<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（[[649年]]）2月条。以下、特に記さない限り『日本書紀』の該当年月条による。</ref>。前の13階制の[[小黒]]が[[小乙上]]と小乙下に分割されたうちの一つである。小乙下は19階中18位で、最下位は[[立身]]であった。
天智天皇3年（[[664年]]）2月9日の冠位26階の制で、小乙は上・中・下に三分されることになった。小乙下は26階中24階で下から3番目になり、上は[[小乙中]]、下は[[大建]]となった。
天武天皇14年（[[685年]]）に冠位の命名方法が一新し、そのとき小乙下も廃止になった
== 叙位された人物 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では三つの記事でこの冠位を授かった人物が現れる。[[中臣間人老]]は、[[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月の[[遣唐使]]の一員である。[[沙尼具那]]と[[青蒜]]は[[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）の[[阿倍比羅夫]]北征で降った[[蝦夷]]で、都に上って饗応され、冠位を授けられた。[[都岐沙羅柵]]と[[渟足柵]]の[[柵造]]も同じ冠位になった。遣多禰島小使の[[上光父]]は、大使で[[大乙下]]の[[倭馬飼部連]]とともに[[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）に[[多禰国|多禰島]]に派遣された。
『[[正倉院文書]]』の中には、小乙下の[[他田日奉部忍]]が[[孝徳天皇]]の時代に[[下総国]][[海上郡]]の[[少領]]だったと述べるものがある<ref>「他田日奉部神護解」、『大日本古文書』第3巻149-150頁。</ref>。また『[[常陸国風土記]]』には、[[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）に[[大建]]の那珂[[国造]][[壬生夫子]]とともに[[行方郡]]新設に携わった茨木国造の[[壬生麿]]が、小乙下とある<ref>『常陸国風土記』行方郡の項。</ref>。
*[[他田日奉部忍]] - [[孝徳天皇]]代（[[645年]] - [[654年]]）。[[海上郡]][[少領]]。 *[[壬生麿]] - [[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）見。茨木国造。 *[[中臣間人老]] - [[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月見。遣唐判官。 *[[沙尼具那]] - [[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）7月4日叙位。[[渟代郡]][[大領]]。 *[[青蒜]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[津軽郡]][[少領]]。 *不詳 - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[都岐沙羅柵]]造。 *[[大伴稲積]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[渟足柵]]造。 *[[上光父]] - [[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）11月23日見。遣多禰島小使。
== 木簡に記された冠位 == [[飛鳥京跡]]から出土した[[木簡]]の中に2つ、小乙下と記されたものがある。一つは1975年度の第51調査で見つかったもので、「小乙下階」とあった。一緒に「[[小山上]]」「[[大花下]]」と記された木簡も発見され、大花下の存続時期から、[[649年]]から[[664年]]の間というところまで絞り込める<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」100-102頁。</ref>。
もう一つは削り屑に「小乙下」とあるもので、1984年度の第104次調査で見付かった。「[[大乙下]]」、「辛巳年」「閏月」といった削り屑も一緒に出た。天武天皇10年（[[681年]]）の干支が辛巳年で閏月も持つ<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」104頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *東京帝国大学史料編纂掛『[[大日本古文書]]』第3巻、東京帝国大学、1902年。 *仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」、平野邦雄・鈴木靖民・編『木簡が語る古代史』上（都の変遷と暮らし）、吉川弘文館、1996年、ISBN 4-642-07492-9。
== 関連項目 == *[[冠位・位階制度の変遷]] [[Category:日本の位階|しようおつけ]]

revision.comment: [[冠位十九階]]等にリンク

== 叙位された人物 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では三つの記事でこの冠位を授かった人物が現れる。[[中臣間人老]]は、[[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月の[[遣唐使]]の一員である。[[沙尼具那]]と[[青蒜]]は[[皇極天皇|斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）の[[阿倍比羅夫]]北征で降った[[蝦夷]]で、都に上って饗応され、冠位を授けられた。[[都岐沙羅柵]]と[[渟足柵]]の[[柵造]]も同じ冠位になった。遣多禰島小使の[[上光父]]は、大使で[[大乙下]]の[[倭馬飼部連]]とともに[[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）に[[多禰国|多禰島]]に派遣された。
『[[正倉院文書]]』の中には、小乙下の[[他田日奉部忍]]が[[孝徳天皇]]の時代に[[下総国]][[海上郡]]の[[少領]]だったと述べるものがある<ref>「他田日奉部神護解」、『大日本古文書』第3巻149-150頁。</ref>。また『[[常陸国風土記]]』には、[[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）に[[大建]]の那珂[[国造]][[壬生夫子]]とともに[[行方郡 (茨城県)|行方郡]]新設に携わった茨木国造の[[壬生麿]]が、小乙下とある<ref>『常陸国風土記』行方郡の項。</ref>。
*[[他田日奉部忍]] - [[孝徳天皇]]代（[[645年]] - [[654年]]）。[[海上郡]][[少領]]。 *[[壬生麿]] - [[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）見。茨木国造。 *[[中臣間人老]] - 白雉5年（[[654年]]）2月見。遣唐判官。 *[[沙尼具那]] - [[皇極天皇|斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）7月4日叙位。[[渟代郡]][[大領]]。 *[[青蒜]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[津軽郡 (陸奥国)|津軽郡]][[少領]]。 *不詳 - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[都岐沙羅柵]]造。 *[[大伴稲積]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[渟足柵]]造。 *[[上光父]] - [[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）11月23日見。遣多禰島小使。
== 木簡に記された冠位 == [[飛鳥京跡]]から出土した[[木簡]]の中に2つ、小乙下と記されたものがある。一つは1975年度の第51調査で見つかったもので、「小乙下階」とあった。一緒に「[[小山上]]」「[[大花下]]」と記された木簡も発見され、大花下の存続時期から、[[649年]]から[[664年]]の間というところまで絞り込める<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」100-102頁。</ref>。
もう一つは削り屑に「小乙下」とあるもので、1984年度の第104次調査で見付かった。「[[大乙下]]」、「辛巳年」「閏月」といった削り屑も一緒に出た。天武天皇10年（[[681年]]）の干支が辛巳年で閏月も持つ<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」104頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *東京帝国大学史料編纂掛『[[大日本古文書]]』第3巻、東京帝国大学、1902年。 *仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」、平野邦雄・鈴木靖民・編『木簡が語る古代史』上（都の変遷と暮らし）、吉川弘文館、1996年、ISBN 4-642-07492-9。
== 関連項目 == *[[冠位・位階制度の変遷]] [[Category:日本の位階|しようおつけ]]

revision.comment: /* 概要 */ 単純で大きな誤りを訂正
revision.text:
'''小乙下'''（しょうおつげ）は、[[649年]]から[[685年]]まで日本で用いられた[[冠位]]である。[[664年]]を境に順位が変わり、下から2番目か3番目にあたる低い冠位であった。上は[[小乙上]]から[[小乙中]]、下は[[立身]]から[[大建]]に変わった。

天武天皇14年（[[685年]]）の[[冠位四十八階]]では冠位の命名方法が一新し、小乙下は廃止になった。
== 叙位された人物 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では三つの記事でこの冠位を授かった人物が現れる。[[中臣間人老]]は、[[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月の[[遣唐使]]の一員である。[[沙尼具那]]と[[青蒜]]は[[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）の[[阿倍比羅夫]]北征で降った[[蝦夷]]で、都に上って饗応され、冠位を授けられた。[[都岐沙羅柵]]と[[渟足柵]]の[[柵造]]も同じ冠位になった。遣多禰島小使の[[上光父]]は、大使で[[大乙下]]の[[倭馬飼部連]]とともに[[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）に[[多禰国|多禰島]]に派遣された。
『[[正倉院文書]]』の中にある「他田日奉部直神護解」には、[[他田日奉神護|神護]]の祖父で小乙下の忍が、[[孝徳天皇]]の時代に[[下総国]][[海上郡]]の[[少領]]だったと書かれている<ref>『大日本古文書』第3巻149-150頁。</ref>。また『[[常陸国風土記]]』には、[[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）に[[大建]]の[[仲国造|那珂国造]][[壬生夫子]]とともに[[行方郡 (茨城県)|行方郡]]新設に携わった[[茨城国造]]の[[壬生麿]]が、小乙下とある<ref>『常陸国風土記』行方郡の項。</ref>。
*[[他田日奉部忍]] - [[孝徳天皇]]代（[[645年]] - [[654年]]）。[[海上郡]][[少領]]。 *[[壬生麿]] - [[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）見。茨城国造。 *[[中臣間人老]] - 白雉5年（[[654年]]）2月見。遣唐判官。 *[[沙尼具那]] - [[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）7月4日叙位。[[渟代郡]][[大領]]。 *[[青蒜]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[津軽郡 (陸奥国)|津軽郡]][[少領]]。 *不詳 - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[都岐沙羅柵]]造。 *[[大伴稲積]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[渟足柵]]造。 *[[上光父]] - [[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）11月23日見。遣多禰島小使。
== 木簡に記された冠位 == [[飛鳥京跡]]から出土した[[木簡]]の中に2つ、小乙下と記されたものがある。一つは1975年度の第51調査で見つかったもので、「小乙下階」とあった。一緒に「[[小山上]]」「[[大花下]]」と記された木簡も発見され、大花下の存続時期から、[[649年]]から[[664年]]の間というところまで絞り込める<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」100-102頁。</ref>。
もう一つは削り屑に「小乙下」とあるもので、1984年度の第104次調査で見付かった。「[[大乙下]]」、「辛巳年」「閏月」といった削り屑も一緒に出た。天武天皇10年（[[681年]]）の干支が辛巳年で閏月も持つ<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」104頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *東京帝国大学史料編纂掛『[[大日本古文書]]』第3巻、東京帝国大学、1902年。 *仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」、平野邦雄・鈴木靖民・編『木簡が語る古代史』上（都の変遷と暮らし）、吉川弘文館、1996年、ISBN 4-642-07492-9。
== 関連項目 == *[[冠位・位階制度の変遷]]

revision.text:
'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|rank within the cap rank system|ja|冠位}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649年}} to {{ill|685|ja|685年}}. The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664年}}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - **{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}**: Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - **{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}**: Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - **{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}**: Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - **{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}**: An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - **{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}**: Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - **{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}**: Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - **{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}**: Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷}}
{{デフォルトソート:しょうおつげ}} [[Category:Cap rank system]] [[ja:小乙下]]

== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|rank within the cap rank system|ja|冠位}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649年}} to {{ill|685|ja|685年}}. The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664年}}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.

== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] to [[Category:Qqqqq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|rank within the cap rank system|ja|冠位}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649年}} to {{ill|685|ja|685年}}. The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664年}}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}

== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks}}

'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|rank within the cap rank system|ja|冠位|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|lt=Crown rank}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649年}} to {{ill|685|ja|685年}}. The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664年}}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks}}

'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|rank within the cap rank system|ja|冠位|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|lt=Crown rank}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649年}} to {{ill|685|ja|685年}}. The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664年}}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks}}

The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664年}}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: - '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. - '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. - '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. - '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. - '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. - '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). - '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "- '''" to "* '''"
revision.text:
'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|rank within the cap rank system|ja|冠位|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|lt=Crown rank}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649年}} to {{ill|685|ja|685年}}. The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664年}}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: * '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. * '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. * '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. * '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. * '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. * '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). * '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks}}

'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|rank within the cap rank system|ja|冠位|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|lt=Crown rank}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649年}} to {{ill|685|ja|685年}}. The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664年}}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（649年）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: * '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. * '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. * '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. * '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. * '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. * '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). * '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks}}

In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: * '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. * '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. * '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. * '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. * '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. * '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). * '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks}}

revision.text:
'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|rank within the cap rank system|ja|冠位|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|lt=Crown rank}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649th year }} to {{ill|685|ja|685th year }}. The rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664th year }}, making it either the second or third lowest rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}, it later ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建}}.
== Overview == The rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒}} rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5th year （649th year ）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: * '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. * '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. * '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. * '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. * '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. * '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). * '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階|12=draft}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階|12=draft}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge rank: * '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍|12=draft}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡|12=draft}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. * '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿|12=draft}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉|12=draft}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. * '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老|12=draft}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. * '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那|12=draft}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. * '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜|12=draft}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. * '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積|12=draft}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). * '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父|12=draft}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡|12=draft}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史|12=draft}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|12=draft}}

14 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:小乙下 up to rev 76169873
revision.text:
'''小乙下'''（しょうおつげ）は、[[649年]]から[[685年]]まで日本で用いられた[[冠位]]である。[[664年]]を境に順位が変わり、下から2番目か3番目にあたる低い冠位であった。上は[[小乙上]]から[[小乙中]]、下は[[立身]]から[[大建]]に変わった。
== 概要 == [[大化]]5年2月に、[[冠位十九階]]の制で導入された<ref>『日本書紀』大化5年（[[649年]]）2月条。以下、特に記さない限り『日本書紀』の該当年月条による。</ref>。前の[[七色十三階冠]]の[[小黒]]が[[小乙上]]と小乙下に分割されたうちの一つである。小乙下は19階中18位で、最下位は[[立身]]であった。
天智天皇3年（[[664年]]）2月9日の[[冠位二十六階]]制で、小乙は上・中・下に三分されることになった。小乙下は26階中24階で下から3番目になり、上は[[小乙中]]、下は[[大建]]となった。
天武天皇14年（[[685年]]）の[[冠位四十八階]]では冠位の命名方法が一新し、小乙下は廃止になった。
== 叙位された人物 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では三つの記事でこの冠位を授かった人物が現れる。[[中臣間人老]]は、[[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）2月の[[遣唐使]]の一員である。[[沙尼具那]]と[[青蒜]]は[[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）の[[阿倍比羅夫]]北征で降った[[蝦夷]]で、都に上って饗応され、冠位を授けられた。[[都岐沙羅柵]]と[[渟足柵]]の[[柵造]]も同じ冠位になった。遣多禰島小使の[[上光父]]は、大使で[[大乙下]]の[[倭馬飼部連]]とともに[[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）に[[多禰国|多禰島]]に派遣された。
『[[正倉院文書]]』の中にある「他田日奉部直神護解」には、[[他田日奉神護|神護]]の祖父で小乙下の忍が、[[孝徳天皇]]の時代に[[下総国]][[海上郡]]の[[少領]]だったと書かれている<ref>『大日本古文書』第3巻149-150頁。</ref>。また『[[常陸国風土記]]』には、[[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）に[[大建]]の[[仲国造|那珂国造]][[壬生夫子]]とともに[[行方郡 (茨城県)|行方郡]]新設に携わった[[茨城国造]]の[[壬生麿]]が、小乙下とある<ref>『常陸国風土記』行方郡の項。</ref>。
*[[他田日奉部忍]] - [[孝徳天皇]]代（[[645年]] - [[654年]]）。[[海上郡]][[少領]]。 *[[壬生麿]] - [[白雉]]4年（[[653年]]）見。茨城国造。 *[[中臣間人老]] - 白雉5年（[[654年]]）2月見。遣唐判官。 *[[沙尼具那]] - [[斉明天皇]]4年（[[658年]]）7月4日叙位。[[渟代郡]][[大領]]。 *[[青蒜]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[津軽郡 (陸奥国)|津軽郡]][[少領]]。 *不詳 - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[都岐沙羅柵]]造。 *[[大伴稲積]] - 斉明天皇4年（658年）7月4日叙位。[[渟足柵]]造。 *[[上光父]] - [[天武天皇]]8年（[[679年]]）11月23日見。遣多禰島小使。
== 木簡に記された冠位 == [[飛鳥京跡]]から出土した[[木簡]]の中に2つ、小乙下と記されたものがある。一つは1975年度の第51調査で見つかったもので、「小乙下階」とあった。一緒に「[[小山上]]」「[[大花下]]」と記された木簡も発見され、大花下の存続時期から、[[649年]]から[[664年]]の間というところまで絞り込める<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」100-102頁。</ref>。
もう一つは削り屑に「小乙下」とあるもので、1984年度の第104次調査で見付かった。「[[大乙下]]」、「辛巳年」「閏月」といった削り屑も一緒に出た。天武天皇10年（[[681年]]）の干支が辛巳年で閏月も持つ<ref>仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」104頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *東京帝国大学史料編纂掛『[[大日本古文書]]』第3巻、東京帝国大学、1902年。 *仁藤敦史「飛鳥・藤原の都」、平野邦雄・鈴木靖民・編『木簡が語る古代史』上（都の変遷と暮らし）、吉川弘文館、1996年、ISBN 4-642-07492-9。
== 関連項目 == *[[冠位・位階制度の変遷]]
{{デフォルトソート:しようおつけ}} [[Category:冠位制度]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[小乙下]] to [[Koetsuga]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

revision.text:
'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|Rank within the cap Rank system|ja|冠位|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|lt=Crown Rank|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank within the cap Rank system|13=User:Immanuelle/Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|qq=}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/649|qq=}} to {{ill|685|ja|685th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/685|qq=}}. The Rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/664|qq=}}, making it either the second or third lowest Rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōotsujō|qq=}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Risshin|qq=}}, it later Ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōotsuchū|qq=}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiken|qq=}}.
== Overview == The Rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika|qq=}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/19 Cap Rank System|qq=}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkoku|qq=}} Rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōotsujō|qq=}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5th year （649th year ）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th Rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Risshin|qq=}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/26 Cap Rank System|qq=}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th Rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The Rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/48 Cap Rank System|qq=}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge Rank: * '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tajihi no Hinamori|qq=}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijo District|qq=}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. * '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mibu no Maro|qq=}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuchi|qq=}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. * '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Hitooshio|qq=}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. * '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saniguna|qq=}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. * '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aobiru|qq=}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. * '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Inazumi|qq=}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). * '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ue no Mitsuchichi|qq=}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the Rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Palace Site|qq=}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge Rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated Ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|qq=}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Transition of the Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Transition of the Cap and Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|qq=}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]]
[[Category:Cap Rank system]]
}}
[[sn:Koetsuga]] [[ja:小乙下]] [[en:Koetsuga]]
==Merged second translation== '''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a [[Rank]] in the Japanese [[court Rank system]] used between [[649]] and [[685]]. The Rank's position changed with time: it was the second lowest Rank until [[664]], and after that, it became the third lowest. It was below [[Shōotsujō|Shōotsujō]] and [[Shōotsuchū|Shōotsuchū]] and above [[Risshin|Risshin]] or [[Daiken|Daiken]] depending on the period.
== Overview ==
Shōotsuge was introduced in [[Taika]] 5 (649 CE) as part of the [[nineteen-Rank system]].<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Taika 5 (649 CE), 2nd month. Unless otherwise noted, references to events in the ''Nihon Shoki'' follow the dates provided therein.</ref> It was created by splitting the previous [[Seven Colors and Thirteen Ranks|Shikishijūsankai]] Rank of [[Kokuro|Kokuro]] into [[Shōotsujō|Shōotsujō]] and Shōotsuge. Shōotsuge was the 18th Rank out of 19, with [[Risshin|Risshin]] being the lowest.
In the 3rd year of [[Emperor Tenji]] (664 CE), the [[twenty-six-Rank system]] was implemented, dividing Shōotsu into three Ranks: [[Shōotsujō|Shōotsujō]], [[Shōotsuchū|Shōotsuchū]], and Shōotsuge. Under this system, Shōotsuge became the 24th Rank out of 26, below [[Shōotsuchū|Shōotsuchū]] and above [[Daiken|Daiken]].
In the 14th year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (685 CE), the [[forty-eight-Rank system]] was introduced, and the naming conventions for Ranks were overhauled, resulting in the abolition of Shōotsuge.
== Individuals Awarded Shōotsuge ==
Three records in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mention individuals granted this Rank. [[Nakatomi no Hataru no Oi]] was an envoy to [[Tang China]] in [[Hakuchi]] 5 (654 CE). [[Saniguna]] and [[Aobiru]] were [[Emishi]] chieftains who surrendered to [[Abe no Hirafu]]'s northern expedition in [[Saimei]] 4 (658 CE) and were brought to the capital, entertained, and granted court Ranks. The [[Fort Lords]] of [[Tukisara Castle|Tsukisara Fort]] and [[Numusari Castle|Numusari Fort]] were also assigned this Rank. [[Kamimitu]] served as a junior envoy on a mission to the [[Tanegashima|island of Tane]] in [[Tenmu]] 8 (679 CE), under [[Yamabaikainomuraji|Yamabaikainomuraji]] of [[Ōotsuge]] Rank.
The ''[[Shōsōin documents]]'' mention a Shōotsuge named [[Othera no Hihohe no Oshikami|Oshikami]], whose grandfather served as a minor official in [[Shimousa Province]] during [[Emperor Kōtoku]]'s reign.<ref>''Dai Nihon Komonjo,'' Vol. 3, pp. 149–150.</ref> Another record in the ''[[Hitachi Fudoki]]'' describes [[Mibu no Maro]], an official from [[Ibaraki Province]], who worked with [[Naka Province Chief|Naka Province Chief]] [[Mibu no Fukiko]] to establish [[Namegata District]] in [[Hakuchi]] 4 (653 CE).<ref>''Hitachi Fudoki,'' Namegata District section.</ref>
=== Individuals Known to Hold Shōotsuge === * {{ill|Othera no Hihohe no Oshikami|ja|他田日奉部忍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Othera no Hihohe no Oshikami|qq=}} - During [[Emperor Kōtoku]]'s reign (645–654 CE), a minor official in [[Kaijo District]]. * {{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mibu no Maro|qq=}} - Recorded in Hakuchi 4 (653 CE) as [[Ibaraki Province Chief]]. * {{ill|Nakatomi no Hataru no Oi|ja|中臣間人老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Hataru no Oi|qq=}} - Recorded in Hakuchi 5 (654 CE), served as a Tang China envoy. * {{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saniguna|qq=}} - Promoted on Saimei 4 (658 CE), served as chief of [[Numusari Fort]]. * {{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aobiru|qq=}} - Promoted on Saimei 4 (658 CE), served as minor official of [[Tsukisara Castle]]. * {{ill|Fort Lord|ja|柵造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fort Lord|qq=}} - Promoted on Saimei 4 (658 CE), chief of [[Tsukisara Castle]]. * {{ill|Ōtomo no Inatsumi|ja|大伴稲積|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Inatsumi|qq=}} - Promoted on Saimei 4 (658 CE), chief of [[Numusari Castle]]. * {{ill|Kamimitu|ja|上光父|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimitu|qq=}} - Recorded in Tenmu 8 (679 CE) as junior envoy to Tanegashima.
== Wooden Tablets with Shōotsuge Markings ==
Two wooden tablets from the [[Asuka Palace site]] bear inscriptions of the Rank Shōotsuge. One, discovered during the 51st excavation in 1975, is labeled "Shōotsuge Rank" alongside tablets inscribed with [[Shōsanjō|Shōsanjō]] and [[Ōhanage|Ōhanage]]. Based on the timeframe of the latter Rank, the tablet dates between [[649]] and [[664]].<ref>Nitō, Atsushi. "Capitals of Asuka and Fujiwara," pp. 100–102.</ref>
The second, found during the 104th excavation in 1984, was inscribed on shavings along with "Ōotsuge," "Year of Xin-Si," and "Intercalary Month." The year Xin-Si corresponds to [[Tenmu]] 10 (681 CE), which also had an intercalary month.<ref>Nitō, Atsushi. "Capitals of Asuka and Fujiwara," p. 104.</ref>
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == * Tokyo Imperial University Archives, ''[[Dai Nihon Komonjo]]'', Vol. 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. * Nitō, Atsushi. "Capitals of Asuka and Fujiwara," in Kunio Hirano and Yasumitsu Suzuki, eds., ''Chronicles Told by Wooden Tablets'' Vol. 1 (Capitals and Daily Life), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996. ISBN 4-642-07492-9. == See Also == * {{ill|History of the court Rank and title system|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of the court Rank and title system|13=User:Immanuelle/Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|qq=}}

revision.text:
'''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a {{ill|Rank within the cap Rank system|ja|冠位|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|lt=Crown Rank|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank within the cap Rank system|13=User:Immanuelle/Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|qq=}} used in Japan from {{ill|649|ja|649th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/649|qq=}} to {{ill|685|ja|685th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/685|qq=}}. The Rank's position within the hierarchy changed after {{ill|664|ja|664th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/664|qq=}}, making it either the second or third lowest Rank. Initially positioned below {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōotsujō|qq=}} and above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Risshin|qq=}}, it later Ranked below {{ill|Shōotsuchū|ja|小乙中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōotsuchū|qq=}} and above {{ill|Daiken|ja|大建|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiken|qq=}}.
== Overview == The Rank was introduced in {{ill|Taika|ja|大化|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika|qq=}} 5 (649) as part of the {{ill|19 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位十九階|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/19 Cap Rank System|qq=}}, splitting the former {{ill|Shōkoku|ja|小黒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkoku|qq=}} Rank into {{ill|Shōotsujō|ja|小乙上|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōotsujō|qq=}} and Shōotsuge<ref>『日本書紀』大化5th year （649th year ）2月条。</ref>. Shōotsuge was the 18th Rank out of 19, positioned just above {{ill|Risshin|ja|立身|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Risshin|qq=}}.
In the 3rd year of Emperor Tenji's reign (664), the {{ill|26 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/26 Cap Rank System|qq=}} was established, subdividing Shōotsu into three levels: upper, middle, and lower. Shōotsuge became the 24th Rank out of 26, just above Daiken.
The Rank was abolished in the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (685) with the introduction of the {{ill|48 Cap Rank System|ja|冠位四十八階|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/48 Cap Rank System|qq=}}.
== Individuals Holding the Rank == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} mentions several individuals who held the Shōotsuge Rank: * '''{{ill|Tajihi no Hinamori|ja|他田日奉部忍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tajihi no Hinamori|qq=}}''': Served as a minor official (Shōryō) of {{ill|Kaijo District|ja|海上郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijo District|qq=}} in Shimōsa Province during Emperor Kōtoku's reign. * '''{{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mibu no Maro|qq=}}''': Recognized in {{ill|Hakuchi|ja|白雉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuchi|qq=}} 4 (653) as Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, involved in founding Namekata District. * '''{{ill|Nakatomi no Hitooshio|ja|中臣間人老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Hitooshio|qq=}}''': Mentioned in Hakuchi 5 (654) as part of a Tang envoy delegation. * '''{{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saniguna|qq=}}''': An Emishi chieftain who surrendered in Saimei 4 (658) and became a district official. * '''{{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aobiru|qq=}}''': Another Emishi leader from Saimei 4 (658), later appointed as a minor official in Tsugaru. - An unidentified individual in Saimei 4 (658), tasked with constructing the Tsugisara Palisade. * '''{{ill|Ōtomo no Inazumi|ja|大伴稲積|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Inazumi|qq=}}''': Recognized for constructing the Nunozashi Palisade in Saimei 4 (658). * '''{{ill|Ue no Mitsuchichi|ja|上光父|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ue no Mitsuchichi|qq=}}''': Assigned to Tanegashima as a minor envoy in Tenmu 8 (679).
== Shōotsuge in Mokkan (Wooden Tablets) == Two mokkan (wooden tablets) mentioning the Rank Shōotsuge have been excavated from the {{ill|Asuka Palace Site|ja|飛鳥京跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Palace Site|qq=}}: - One found in the 51st excavation (1975) contains the phrase "Shōotsuge Rank" and is dated to 649–664 based on associated Ranks. - Another found in the 104th excavation (1984) includes the term "Shōotsuge" along with references to "Daiken" and the year "辛巳" (681).
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== References == - Tokyo Imperial University Historical Compilation Department, 『大日本古文書』 Volume 3, Tokyo Imperial University, 1902. - Nito Atsushi, "Asuka and Fujiwara Capitals," in {{ill|The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|ja|木簡が語る古代史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Ancient History Told by Wooden Tablets|qq=}}, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996, ISBN 4-642-07492-9.

[[sn:Koetsuga]] [[ja:小乙下]] [[en:Koetsuga]]
==Merged second translation== '''Shōotsuge''' (小乙下) was a [[Rank]] in the Japanese [[court Rank system]] used between [[649]] and [[685]]. The Rank's position changed with time: it was the second lowest Rank until [[664]], and after that, it became the third lowest. It was below [[Shōotsujō|Shōotsujō]] and [[Shōotsuchū|Shōotsuchū]] and above [[Risshin|Risshin]] or [[Daiken|Daiken]] depending on the period.
== Overview ==
Shōotsuge was introduced in [[Taika]] 5 (649 CE) as part of the [[nineteen-Rank system]].<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Taika 5 (649 CE), 2nd month. Unless otherwise noted, references to events in the ''Nihon Shoki'' follow the dates provided therein.</ref> It was created by splitting the previous [[Seven Colors and Thirteen Ranks|Shikishijūsankai]] Rank of [[Kokuro|Kokuro]] into [[Shōotsujō|Shōotsujō]] and Shōotsuge. Shōotsuge was the 18th Rank out of 19, with [[Risshin|Risshin]] being the lowest.
In the 3rd year of [[Emperor Tenji]] (664 CE), the [[twenty-six-Rank system]] was implemented, dividing Shōotsu into three Ranks: [[Shōotsujō|Shōotsujō]], [[Shōotsuchū|Shōotsuchū]], and Shōotsuge. Under this system, Shōotsuge became the 24th Rank out of 26, below [[Shōotsuchū|Shōotsuchū]] and above [[Daiken|Daiken]].
In the 14th year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (685 CE), the [[forty-eight-Rank system]] was introduced, and the naming conventions for Ranks were overhauled, resulting in the abolition of Shōotsuge.
== Individuals Awarded Shōotsuge ==
Three records in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mention individuals granted this Rank. [[Nakatomi no Hataru no Oi]] was an envoy to [[Tang China]] in [[Hakuchi]] 5 (654 CE). [[Saniguna]] and [[Aobiru]] were [[Emishi]] chieftains who surrendered to [[Abe no Hirafu]]'s northern expedition in [[Saimei]] 4 (658 CE) and were brought to the capital, entertained, and granted court Ranks. The [[Fort Lords]] of [[Tukisara Castle|Tsukisara Fort]] and [[Numusari Castle|Numusari Fort]] were also assigned this Rank. [[Kamimitu]] served as a junior envoy on a mission to the [[Tanegashima|island of Tane]] in [[Tenmu]] 8 (679 CE), under [[Yamabaikainomuraji|Yamabaikainomuraji]] of [[Ōotsuge]] Rank.
The ''[[Shōsōin documents]]'' mention a Shōotsuge named [[Othera no Hihohe no Oshikami|Oshikami]], whose grandfather served as a minor official in [[Shimousa Province]] during [[Emperor Kōtoku]]'s reign.<ref>''Dai Nihon Komonjo,'' Vol. 3, pp. 149–150.</ref> Another record in the ''[[Hitachi Fudoki]]'' describes [[Mibu no Maro]], an official from [[Ibaraki Province]], who worked with [[Naka Province Chief|Naka Province Chief]] [[Mibu no Fukiko]] to establish [[Namegata District]] in [[Hakuchi]] 4 (653 CE).<ref>''Hitachi Fudoki,'' Namegata District section.</ref>
=== Individuals Known to Hold Shōotsuge === * {{ill|Othera no Hihohe no Oshikami|ja|他田日奉部忍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Othera no Hihohe no Oshikami|qq=}} - During [[Emperor Kōtoku]]'s reign (645–654 CE), a minor official in [[Kaijo District]]. * {{ill|Mibu no Maro|ja|壬生麿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mibu no Maro|qq=}} - Recorded in Hakuchi 4 (653 CE) as [[Ibaraki Province Chief]]. * {{ill|Nakatomi no Hataru no Oi|ja|中臣間人老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Hataru no Oi|qq=}} - Recorded in Hakuchi 5 (654 CE), served as a Tang China envoy. * {{ill|Saniguna|ja|沙尼具那|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saniguna|qq=}} - Promoted on Saimei 4 (658 CE), served as chief of [[Numusari Fort]]. * {{ill|Aobiru|ja|青蒜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aobiru|qq=}} - Promoted on Saimei 4 (658 CE), served as minor official of [[Tsukisara Castle]]. * {{ill|Fort Lord|ja|柵造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fort Lord|qq=}} - Promoted on Saimei 4 (658 CE), chief of [[Tsukisara Castle]]. * {{ill|Ōtomo no Inatsumi|ja|大伴稲積|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Inatsumi|qq=}} - Promoted on Saimei 4 (658 CE), chief of [[Numusari Castle]]. * {{ill|Kamimitu|ja|上光父|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimitu|qq=}} - Recorded in Tenmu 8 (679 CE) as junior envoy to Tanegashima.
== Wooden Tablets with Shōotsuge Markings ==
Two wooden tablets from the [[Asuka Palace site]] bear inscriptions of the Rank Shōotsuge. One, discovered during the 51st excavation in 1975, is labeled "Shōotsuge Rank" alongside tablets inscribed with [[Shōsanjō|Shōsanjō]] and [[Ōhanage|Ōhanage]]. Based on the timeframe of the latter Rank, the tablet dates between [[649]] and [[664]].<ref>Nitō, Atsushi. "Capitals of Asuka and Fujiwara," pp. 100–102.</ref>
The second, found during the 104th excavation in 1984, was inscribed on shavings along with "Ōotsuge," "Year of Xin-Si," and "Intercalary Month." The year Xin-Si corresponds to [[Tenmu]] 10 (681 CE), which also had an intercalary month.<ref>Nitō, Atsushi. "Capitals of Asuka and Fujiwara," p. 104.</ref>

"Capitals of Asuka and Fujiwara," in Kunio Hirano and Yasumitsu Suzuki, eds., ''Chronicles Told by Wooden Tablets'' Vol. 1 (Capitals and Daily Life), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996. ISBN 4-642-07492-9. == See Also == * {{ill|History of the court Rank and title system|ja|冠位・位階制度の変遷|1=Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of the court Rank and title system|13=User:Immanuelle/Transition of Crown and Court Ranks|qq=}}
